# Course Overview

`GRA 4164 Text as Data` is an English-first course in LearnForge
with reusable concepts, exercises, and lecture collections.

The course explores how textual data can be used in quantitative analysis — Natural
Language Processing (NLP) for business. With the rapid growth of data from social
media, news articles, corporate filings, and reports, extracting actionable insights
from text is increasingly essential for finance, business intelligence, and
data-driven decision-making.

## Knowledge Goals

- Understand how NLP can be strategically integrated into business processes to
  improve efficiencies and gain competitive advantages.
- Discuss and differentiate between NLP methodologies, identifying their strengths
  and weaknesses in relation to different business cases.
- Know the basic steps and techniques for preparing text-based data for analysis,
  including tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, and feature selection.
- Know how to represent text numerically using bag-of-words, TF-IDF, PMI, and
  word embeddings.
- Understand at an intuitive level how n-gram and character-level language models
  work and how they relate to Large Language Models.
- Know the attention mechanism, the Transformer architecture, and why they are
  central to modern LLMs.

## Skills Goals

- Select and apply relevant NLP tools and algorithms in Python.
- Create empirical models using text as data and apply them to business problems.
- Build workflows using bag-of-words methods, topic modelling, and text
  classification.
- Work with word embeddings and understand their practical applications.
- Use prompt engineering techniques to efficiently interact with pre-trained LLMs.

## General Competence

- Apply text-as-data methods strategically to drive business growth, foster
  innovation, and gain competitive advantage.
- Plan and execute a substantial group project with methodological rigour and
  pragmatic data-science practice.
- Provide informed commentary in projects involving the use or fine-tuning of LLMs
  for different business purposes.

## Planned Content Areas

- Text processing fundamentals: tokenization, stemming, stop-words, regular
  expressions
- Text representation: bag-of-words, TF-IDF, cosine similarity, PMI
- Boolean/dictionary approaches, text regressions, classification, and topic
  modelling
- N-gram language models and learning from prediction
- Word embeddings and Word2Vec
- Introduction to Large Language Models and tokenization
- Attention mechanisms and the Transformer architecture
- LLM input/output, training, and fine-tuning
- Prompt engineering and model alignment

## Teaching Format

Teaching combines lectures with practical working sessions. The course runs over
twelve sessions, including two practical days and a final presentation session.

Students work in groups of up to three to solve four assignments that form a written
report. The report is presented and discussed before final submission.

## Assessment

- Individual school exam: 70%
- Group submission (four assignments compiled into a report): 30%

## Tools and Workflow

- Python is the primary programming language.
- Course materials are delivered through Jupyter notebooks.
- The primary textbook is Jurafsky and Martin, *Speech and Language Processing*
  (3rd edition, available online).
- Course assets are maintained as reusable objects in LearnForge's object model.
